meta.title: World3 — Systems Simulation Explorer
brand.name: World3
brand.subtitle: Systems Simulation Explorer
intro.body_1_html: In 1972, a team of researchers at MIT built World3 — a computer model that simulates how population, industrial output, food production, pollution, and natural resources interact over time. Their findings, published in The Limits to Growth, showed that business-as-usual leads to overshoot and decline.
intro.body_2_html: This tool lets you explore those scenarios yourself. Pick a preset to see what happens under different assumptions about technology, policy, and resources — or tweak individual parameters in the Advanced editor.

model.title: How does the World3 model work?
model.hero_body_html: In 1972, researchers built a computer model of civilization's trajectory. It tracks five things: people, industrial output, food, pollution, and natural resources. Below, we walk through the big questions it answers — and show you exactly what it assumes.
model.references.items.0: Meadows, D. H., Meadows, D. L., Randers, J. & Behrens, W. W. — The Limits to Growth (1972)
model.references.items.1: Meadows, D. L., Behrens, W. W., Meadows, D. H., Naill, R. F., Randers, J. & Zahn, E. — Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World (1974)
model.references.items.2: Meadows, D. H., Randers, J. & Meadows, D. L. — Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update (2005)
model.references.items.4: Nebel, A., Kling, A., Willamowski, R. & Schell, T. — PyWorld3-03 recalibration (2024)
model.references.items.5: Our World in Data — validation proxies
model.assumptions_desc: Every constant below is a starting assumption of the World3 model. They are grouped by sector. You can edit any of them in the Advanced editor.
model.section.pop-capacity.question: Can Earth support 10 billion people?
model.section.pop-capacity.summary: The model says it depends on how fast we consume resources and how much pollution we generate. Population peaks and then declines when food or health services can no longer keep up. In the standard run, population peaks around 2030 at roughly 8.4 billion, then falls as life expectancy drops.
model.section.pop-capacity.concepts.0.de: World3 splits the population into four groups: 0–14, 15–44, 45–64, and 65+. People flow from one cohort to the next as they age. Each cohort has its own mortality rate influenced by food, pollution, health services, and crowding.
model.section.pop-capacity.concepts.1.de: Fertility is driven by desired family size, which depends on income and life expectancy. As industrial output rises, families choose fewer children. A “fertility control effectiveness” factor (0–1) models how well contraception works, improving over time with health services.
model.section.pop-capacity.equations.pre: Population is the sum of four cohort stocks (P1–P4). Each stock changes by inflows from births or maturation and outflows from death or maturation to the next group. Life expectancy is a base value multiplied by four factors.
model.section.pop-capacity.equations.fee: This is a reinforcing loop: higher life expectancy → more people → more industrial output → better health services → higher life expectancy. But pollution and food shortages create balancing loops that eventually dominate.
model.section.pop-capacity.sources.0.tex: Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World, Ch. 2 — Population Sector
model.section.pop-capacity.sources.1.tex: Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update, Ch. 3
model.section.resource-depletion.summary: In the model’s standard run, nonrenewable resources (think fossil fuels, metals, minerals) decline steadily and are about 50% depleted by the 2020s. As they get scarce, more and more capital is diverted from industry to extraction, which drags down the whole economy. The model doesn’t say we literally “run out” — it says extraction becomes so expensive it chokes growth.
model.section.resource-depletion.concept: Nonrenewable resources in World3 are an aggregate stock representing fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas), minerals, and metals. Renewables like solar, wind, and hydro are NOT included — the model was built before renewables were significant. This is one of its known limitations.
Fraction of capital allocated to obtaining resources (FCAOR)

As resources deplete, the economy must spend more capital on extraction (deeper mines, harder-to-reach deposits). FCAOR rises from ~5% to over 50%, leaving less capital for industry, agriculture, and services. This is the key mechanism that triggers decline.
model.section.resource-depletion.equatio: Resources are a single stock that depletes at a rate proportional to population and per-capita consumption. A technology multiplier can slow depletion.
This is a balancing loop: fewer resources → higher FCAOR → less industrial output → lower consumption rate → slower depletion. But the economy shrinks in the process.
model.section.resource-depletion.sources: Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World, Ch. 5 — Nonrenewable Resource Sector
model.section.technology-rescue.question: Can technology save us?
model.section.technology-rescue.summary: The model tests this by comparing the standard run with an optimistic scenario where technology improves resource efficiency, pollution control, and agricultural yields from 1975 onward. Technology helps — it delays the peak and raises the ceiling — but in most scenarios it doesn’t prevent overshoot on its own. Growth in consumption outpaces the gains.
model.section.technology-rescue.concepts: Technology is modeled through “switch” constants that flip between two table functions at a given year. For example, NRUF switches from table 1 to table 2, representing a sudden improvement in resource usage efficiency. This is admittedly crude — real technology adoption is gradual.

Constants like LFERT, NRUF, PPOLX have two sets of lookup tables. A switch year (e.g., pyear_policy_switch = 1975 in optimistic scenarios) determines when the model transitions from “business-as-usual” tables to “technology-improved” ones.
model.section.technology-rescue.equation: Technology enters through multiplier functions that modify resource usage, pollution generation, and land yield. The switch year determines when improvements begin.

Technology creates a balancing loop against depletion and pollution, but population and consumption growth create reinforcing loops that can overwhelm the gains. The model suggests technology is necessary but not sufficient without demand-side changes.
Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World, Ch. 7 — Policy Scenarios
Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update, Ch. 7 — Technology
model.section.pollution-cost.question: What does pollution really cost?
model.section.pollution-cost.summary: In the standard run, the pollution index stays low until about 2000, then rises exponentially as industrial output peaks. Pollution reduces land fertility (less food) and life expectancy (more deaths). By the time pollution peaks, the damage is already locked in because persistent pollutants take decades to be absorbed.
Long-lived pollutants that accumulate in the environment — heavy metals, synthetic chemicals, plastics, and greenhouse gases. The model uses a single aggregate index rather than tracking individual substances.
World3 was built in 1972, before climate science was mainstream. It models pollution as a generic stock that affects health and agriculture. In validation, CO₂ concentration serves as the empirical proxy for PPOLX, so climate effects are indirectly captured through the pollution index.
The environment absorbs pollution over time, but there’s a delay (AHL70 ≈ 1.5 years at 1970 levels). As pollution rises, the absorption half-life can grow, meaning the environment becomes less able to clean itself — a dangerous positive feedback.
model.section.pollution-cost.equations.p: Persistent pollution accumulates from industrial and agricultural activity and is slowly absorbed by the environment. The pollution index (PPOLX) is the ratio of current pollution to its 1970 reference level.
model.section.pollution-cost.equations.f: Rising pollution → lower land fertility → less food → higher mortality → lower population → less pollution (balancing). But also: rising pollution → longer absorption time → even more pollution (reinforcing). The reinforcing loop dominates during the overshoot phase.
model.section.pollution-cost.sources.0.t: Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World, Ch. 4 — Pollution Sector
model.section.pollution-cost.sources.1.t: Nebel et al. (2024) — CO₂ as validation proxy for PPOL
model.section.food-enough.question: Is there enough food?
model.section.food-enough.summary: In the standard run, food per capita rises until about 2010, then declines as arable land erodes, pollution reduces fertility, and capital is diverted to resource extraction. The model doesn’t predict famine for everyone — it shows that the global average drops below the level needed to sustain life expectancy.
model.section.food-enough.concepts.0.def: Land suitable for growing crops. In World3, arable land can be developed (converted from other uses) or lost to erosion and urban expansion. Once lost, it takes a very long time to recover. The initial stock is about 0.9 billion hectares.
model.section.food-enough.concepts.1.def: How much food is produced per hectare. Depends on agricultural inputs (capital, fertilizer) and land fertility. Pollution degrades land fertility over time. Technology can increase yield, but with diminishing returns.
model.section.food-enough.equations.prea: Food production is land yield times arable land, divided by population for the per-capita figure. Land yield depends on agricultural inputs and fertility.
model.section.food-enough.equations.feed: More agricultural capital → higher yield → more food → lower mortality → more people → more capital needed (reinforcing). But pollution degrades fertility and arable land erodes, creating balancing loops.
model.section.food-enough.sources.0.text: Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World, Ch. 3 — Agriculture Sector
model.section.food-enough.sources.1.text: Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update, Ch. 4
explore.title: Explore a scenario
explore.classic_help: Five core World3 indicators on one chart.
compare.title: Compare scenarios
advanced.title: Advanced: edit constants
advanced.subtitle: Adjust any of the World3 model constants below. The simulation updates automatically as you change values. Each constant controls a specific aspect of the model.
calibrate.title: Calibrate & Validate
calibrate.intro_html: Calibrate model constants and validate simulation output against real-world observations from Our World in Data (OWID).
calibrate.section_desc_html: Fetches observed data from Our World in Data for the chosen entity and reference year, then fits World3 model constants to match the real-world observations. The table below shows which OWID indicator was used for each constant and how confident the mapping is.
validate.section_desc_html: Runs a default World3 simulation and compares each output variable against the corresponding OWID time series. The metrics table shows how well the simulation tracks observed data (RMSE, MAPE, correlation) and which OWID indicator each variable is compared against.

preset.comprehensive-policy.description: Combined technology, population, and agriculture improvements.
preset.doubled-resources.name: Doubled resources
preset.doubled-resources.description: What if the initial nonrenewable resource base were twice as large?
preset.optimistic-technology.name: Optimistic technology
preset.optimistic-technology.description: What if technology halves pollution and doubles resource efficiency?
preset.population-stability.name: Population stability
preset.population-stability.description: What if desired family size drops and population stabilizes earlier?
preset.standard-run.name: Standard run
preset.standard-run.description: Business as usual — all World3 defaults with no policy changes.
variable.fcaor: Fraction capital allocated to obtaining resources
intro.ack.item_0_html: Club of Rome — commissioned the original Limits to Growth study at MIT (1972)
history.title: The Story Behind the Model
history.subtitle: From a controversial 1972 report to the planetary boundaries framework — how The Limits to Growth shaped our understanding of global sustainability.
history.origin.title: 1972: A World Model Is Born
history.origin.body_html: In 1968, Italian industrialist Aurelio Peccei and Scottish scientist Alexander King founded the Club of Rome, a think tank concerned with long-term global challenges. They commissioned a team at MIT — building on Jay Forrester’s pioneering World Dynamics model (1971) — led by Dennis Meadows and Donella Meadows to build a computer simulation of the world system. The result was World3, and its findings were published in 1972 as The Limits to Growth. The book sold over 30 million copies in 30 languages, making it one of the best-selling environmental books in history. Its central message: exponential growth in population and consumption on a finite planet leads to overshoot and collapse — unless society deliberately changes course.
history.backlash.title: The Backlash: “They Got It Wrong”
history.backlash.body_html: Almost immediately, economists and politicians pushed back. Julian Simon called the study “neo-Malthusian nonsense.” William Nordhaus published a critique in 1992 arguing the model was too simplistic. The most common misconception: that the book predicted the world would “run out of resources by 2000.” It never said that. The model explored scenarios — it showed that overshoot dynamics (delayed feedback, exponential growth hitting limits) would cause decline, not that any single resource would hit zero on a fixed date. By the 1990s, the conventional wisdom was that The Limits to Growth had been “debunked.” This narrative persisted for decades.
history.vindication.title: 2008–2014: The Data Catches Up
history.vindication.body_html: In 2008, physicist Graham Turner (paper) at Australia’s CSIRO compared 30 years of real-world data against the original 1972 scenarios. His finding: the “standard run” (business-as-usual) scenario tracked observed data with remarkable accuracy. Population, industrial output, food production, pollution, and resource depletion were all following the trajectory World3 had projected. Turner updated his analysis in 2014 with 40 years of data and the conclusion held. The “debunked” narrative began to unravel. The model had not predicted exact dates or precise numbers — it had captured the structural dynamics of overshoot, and those dynamics were playing out as modeled.
history.updates.title: 2005 & 2022: Revisiting the Limits
history.updates.body_html: In 2005, the original authors published Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update, concluding that humanity had already overshot several critical limits and that the window for a smooth, managed transition was narrowing. By 2022, on the 50th anniversary, Dennis Meadows reflected that the opportunity for a gradual correction had likely passed — the question was no longer whether overshoot would occur, but how societies would adapt to it. In 2023, the Earth Commission’s Safe and Just Earth System Boundaries study confirmed that six of nine planetary boundaries had already been crossed.
history.boundaries.title: Planetary Boundaries: The Modern Framework
history.boundaries.body_html: In 2009, Johan Rockström and a team of Earth system scientists introduced the Planetary Boundaries framework, identifying nine Earth system processes with thresholds that, if crossed, could trigger irreversible environmental change. The nine boundaries — climate change, biodiversity loss, nitrogen and phosphorus flows, ocean acidification, land-use change, freshwater use, ozone depletion, atmospheric aerosols, and chemical pollution — map directly onto the dynamics World3 modeled decades earlier. Where World3 used aggregate stocks (resources, pollution, food, population), Planetary Boundaries gives each limit a name and a measurement. The intellectual lineage is clear: both frameworks say that growth within a finite system encounters hard limits, and that crossing those limits triggers reinforcing feedback loops that are difficult to reverse. The Limits to Growth was the warning. Planetary Boundaries is the diagnosis.
intro.ack.item_7_html: Breaking Down: Collapse — Ep. 4: Overshoot & Limits to Growth (podcast, 2020)
faq.title: Common Misconceptions
faq.subtitle: The Limits to Growth has been widely misquoted and misunderstood since 1972. Here are the most common objections — and what the model actually says.
faq.ran_out.question: "They predicted we'd run out of resources by 2000"
faq.ran_out.answer_html: This is the most persistent myth. The Limits to Growth never predicted that any specific resource would run out on a specific date. The model explored scenarios — it showed that exponential growth in consumption, combined with delayed feedback loops, leads to overshoot: a pattern where demand exceeds sustainable supply, followed by decline. The standard run shows resources becoming critically scarce around the 2020s–2030s, not 2000.
faq.technology_saves.question: "Technology will solve everything"
faq.technology_saves.answer_html: The model explicitly tested this. The "optimistic technology" scenario assumes resource efficiency doubles and pollution is halved from 1975 onward. Result: technology delays the peak and raises the ceiling, but does not prevent overshoot on its own. Growth in consumption outpaces the efficiency gains. The model's conclusion isn't anti-technology — it's that technology is necessary but not sufficient without demand-side changes. You can explore this scenario yourself.
faq.too_simple.question: "The model is too simple to be useful"
faq.too_simple.answer_html: World3 is a systems dynamics model — it captures structural feedback loops, not point predictions. It doesn't try to forecast GDP in 2047 or oil prices in 2030. It identifies the qualitative behavior of a system: does it stabilize, oscillate, or overshoot? This is like how a weather model can tell you "a storm is coming" without predicting exactly which street will flood. Graham Turner's 2008 comparison showed that 30 years of real data tracked the standard run remarkably well — precisely because the model captured the right structural dynamics.
faq.malthus.question: "Malthus was wrong, so this must be wrong too"
faq.malthus.answer_html: Malthus modeled a single exponential (population) hitting a single limit (food). World3 models interconnected feedback loops across five sectors: population, capital, agriculture, resources, and pollution. Overshoot in World3 isn't caused by one resource running out — it's caused by the interaction of multiple stresses: resource depletion diverts capital from industry, which reduces food production, which increases mortality, which eventually reduces population. This is fundamentally different from simple Malthusian arithmetic.
faq.no_collapse_yet.question: "We haven't collapsed yet, so the model is wrong"
faq.no_collapse_yet.answer_html: The standard run shows global population peaking around 2030 and industrial output peaking around 2015, with decline accelerating through the mid-21st century. We are currently at or near the inflection point — not past it. The model never predicted sudden collapse. It predicted a gradual erosion of well-being: declining life expectancy, falling food per capita, rising pollution costs. These trends are consistent with 2020 data (Herrington, 2021). The question isn't whether overshoot has started — it's how societies will respond to it.
intro.book_callout_html: This simulator is based on the World3 model from The Limits to Growth (1972) by Donella Meadows, Dennis Meadows, Jørgen Randers, and William Behrens. If you find these scenarios compelling, read the book — the model is only as meaningful as the assumptions behind it, and the book explains the reasoning in full.
intro.scenario_context_html: The Comprehensive policy scenario is the only one that avoids overshoot. It combines technology improvements with population stabilization and agricultural investment — showing that a sustainable path exists, but requires coordinated action across all sectors.

intro.ack.item_8_html: Forrester, J. W. — World Dynamics (1971), the precursor model that inspired World3
history.cybernetics.title: The Cybernetics Revolution
history.cybernetics.body_html: Cybernetics — the study of feedback and control in systems, pioneered by Norbert Wiener in the 1940s — showed that systems, whether machines, organisms, or societies, could be understood through their feedback loops: how outputs circle back to influence inputs. In the 1950s and 60s, Jay Forrester at MIT translated these ideas into system dynamics — a method for simulating complex systems on computers. His Industrial Dynamics (1961) and Urban Dynamics (1969) demonstrated that counterintuitive behavior emerges from feedback loops: policies that seem helpful can make problems worse, and delays between cause and effect create oscillations and overshoot. Forrester’s World Dynamics (1971) was the first attempt to simulate the entire global system — population, capital, agriculture, resources, and pollution — as one interconnected model. For the first time, humanity had a tool to ask: what happens when exponential growth meets finite planetary limits? World3 built directly on this foundation.
history.systems_thinking.title: Systems Thinking: Donella Meadows’ Legacy
history.systems_thinking.body_html: Donella Meadows was not only the lead author of The Limits to Growth — she became one of the most influential systems thinkers of the 20th century. Her 1999 article Leverage Points: Places to Intervene in a System identified twelve places where small changes can produce large effects — from adjusting parameters (weak leverage) to changing the goals and paradigms of a system (powerful leverage). Her posthumous book Thinking in Systems: A Primer (2008) became the standard introduction to systems dynamics, teaching a generation of researchers, policymakers, and activists to see the world in terms of stocks, flows, feedback loops, and delays. Meadows argued that the most important insight from World3 was not any specific prediction — it was the understanding that exponential growth in a finite system produces overshoot not because of any single failure, but because of the structure of the system itself. Changing that structure requires changing how we think.
